title: Academic & Study English
description: Vocabulary essential for studying, academic writing, research, exams, and university life.

cards.meaning: an outline of topics covered in a course
cards.example: The syllabus lists all topics we will study this semester.

cards.meaning: the subjects and content taught in a school or course
cards.example: The school updated its curriculum to include coding.

cards.meaning: a half-year period of study at a college
cards.example: The spring semester begins in February.

cards.meaning: an educational talk given to a group of students
cards.example: The lecture on biology lasted two hours.

cards.meaning: a small-group teaching session
cards.example: She attended the tutorial after the main lecture.

cards.meaning: a class for discussion and detailed study of a topic
cards.example: The professor led a seminar on modern literature.

cards.meaning: a task or piece of work given to a student
cards.example: I have a 2000-word assignment due next Friday.

cards.meaning: a long research paper for a university degree
cards.example: His thesis focused on climate change in the Arctic.

cards.meaning: an extended written study for a higher degree
cards.example: She spent a year writing her doctoral dissertation.

cards.meaning: a list of books and sources used in a piece of work
cards.example: Include a full bibliography at the end of your essay.

cards.meaning: a reference to a source in academic writing
cards.example: Each citation must match the bibliography entry.

cards.meaning: a source of information used in research
cards.example: Use reliable references to support your arguments.

cards.meaning: copying someone else's work and presenting it as your own
cards.example: Plagiarism is a serious academic offence.

cards.meaning: a short summary of a research paper
cards.example: Read the abstract before deciding to read the full article.

cards.meaning: a proposed explanation to be tested through research
cards.example: Her hypothesis was that sleep improves memory.

cards.meaning: the system of methods used in research
cards.example: Explain your methodology in the research section.

cards.meaning: the final section summarizing findings and outcomes
cards.example: Her conclusion tied together all the key arguments.

cards.meaning: a reason or set of reasons supporting a point
cards.example: Her argument was well-supported by evidence.

cards.meaning: facts used to prove something is true
cards.example: You must provide evidence for every claim you make.

cards.meaning: a detailed examination of something
cards.example: The analysis revealed several unexpected results.

cards.meaning: a detailed evaluation of something's strengths and weaknesses
cards.example: Write a critique of the article, not just a summary.

cards.meaning: evaluation of research by experts in the same field
cards.example: The study passed peer review before being published.

cards.meaning: an early version of a piece of writing
cards.example: Submit your first draft by the end of the week.

cards.meaning: reviewing and improving a piece of writing or studying material
cards.example: She spent the weekend doing revision for her exams.

cards.meaning: to read and correct errors in written work
cards.example: Always proofread your essay before submitting it.

cards.meaning: the latest time by which something must be done
cards.example: The assignment deadline is midnight on Thursday.

cards.meaning: the act of handing in work for assessment
cards.example: Late submissions will not be accepted.

cards.meaning: a mark or score given for academic work
cards.example: She received the top grade in the class.

cards.meaning: the evaluation of a student's knowledge or work
cards.example: The final assessment includes an oral presentation.

cards.meaning: comments given to help improve work
cards.example: The tutor provided detailed feedback on her essay.

cards.meaning: a unit or component of a course
cards.example: I chose a module on environmental science.

cards.meaning: a unit of academic achievement
cards.example: You need 120 credits to complete the degree.

cards.meaning: a required course or condition before taking another
cards.example: Statistics is a prerequisite for this research module.

cards.meaning: an optional course chosen by the student
cards.example: I chose photography as my elective this term.

cards.meaning: financial support given to a student based on merit or need
cards.example: She won a scholarship to study at Oxford.

cards.meaning: a grant for advanced study or research
cards.example: He received a research fellowship for his postdoctoral work.

cards.meaning: a period of work experience in a professional setting
cards.example: She did a summer internship at a marketing firm.

cards.meaning: the fee charged for teaching at a school or college
cards.example: University tuition fees have risen sharply.

cards.meaning: the process of registering for a course or school
cards.example: Enrollment for the autumn semester opens in July.

cards.meaning: an introduction programme for new students
cards.example: Freshers attend orientation during the first week.

cards.meaning: a building with bedrooms for students
cards.example: She lives in the university dormitory.

cards.meaning: the grounds and buildings of a university
cards.example: The campus has excellent sports facilities.

cards.meaning: a building where books and resources are kept for study
cards.example: I spent the afternoon studying in the library.

cards.meaning: an organized collection of information, often digital
cards.example: Use the academic database to find journal articles.

cards.meaning: a publication of scholarly articles in a specific field
cards.example: Her research was published in a medical journal.

cards.meaning: a piece of writing in a newspaper or academic journal
cards.example: Read the article and summarize its main points.

cards.meaning: a book used as a standard source for a subject
cards.example: The textbook covers all the key theories.

cards.meaning: a list of terms with definitions at the end of a text
cards.example: Check the glossary for definitions of technical terms.

cards.meaning: an alphabetical list of topics at the back of a book
cards.example: Use the index to find specific topics quickly.

cards.meaning: extra material added at the end of a document
cards.example: Raw data can be included in an appendix.

cards.meaning: a diagram or image used to illustrate information
cards.example: See Figure 3 for the distribution of results.

cards.meaning: data organised in rows and columns
cards.example: Table 1 shows the comparison of test scores.

cards.meaning: a visual display of information
cards.example: The bar chart shows monthly attendance rates.

cards.meaning: a drawing that explains how something works
cards.example: Study the diagram carefully before answering the question.

cards.meaning: a diagram showing the relationship between data
cards.example: The graph shows a steady increase in results.

cards.meaning: the collection and analysis of numerical data
cards.example: Statistics show that literacy rates are improving.

cards.meaning: facts and figures collected for reference or analysis
cards.example: The data was collected from 200 participants.

cards.meaning: a small group selected to represent a larger group
cards.example: The sample included students from three universities.

cards.meaning: a set of questions used to collect information
cards.example: She designed a survey to measure student satisfaction.

cards.meaning: a set of written questions for research
cards.example: Participants completed a questionnaire about their habits.

cards.meaning: a formal conversation to gather information
cards.example: He conducted interviews with ten teachers.

cards.meaning: watching and recording behaviour or events
cards.example: Classroom observation helped her understand student needs.

cards.meaning: a scientific test done to discover something
cards.example: The experiment proved the hypothesis was correct.

cards.meaning: a factor that can change in an experiment
cards.example: Temperature was the independent variable in the study.

cards.meaning: a group in an experiment that does not receive the treatment
cards.example: The control group received a placebo.

cards.meaning: a relationship or connection between two things
cards.example: There is a strong correlation between exercise and mood.

cards.meaning: a direct cause-and-effect relationship between two things
cards.example: Correlation does not always imply causation.

cards.meaning: a conclusion drawn from evidence and reasoning
cards.example: We can make an inference from the available data.

cards.meaning: a broad conclusion based on specific examples
cards.example: Avoid overgeneralization when interpreting results.

cards.meaning: an unfair preference that affects research results
cards.example: Researcher bias can affect the validity of a study.

cards.meaning: the degree to which a study measures what it claims to
cards.example: The test has high validity for measuring reading ability.

cards.meaning: the consistency of results over repeated tests
cards.example: High reliability means the test gives the same result each time.

cards.meaning: always behaving or performing in the same way
cards.example: Consistency in methodology strengthens your findings.

cards.meaning: the quality of being clear and logically connected
cards.example: Good essays need coherence throughout every paragraph.

cards.meaning: the quality of being clear and easy to understand
cards.example: Write with clarity so your reader can follow your argument.

cards.meaning: expressing ideas clearly without unnecessary words
cards.example: Be concise in your answers; avoid repetition.

cards.meaning: to briefly state the main points of something
cards.example: Summarize the article in no more than 100 words.

cards.meaning: to restate something in different words
cards.example: Paraphrase the quote rather than copying it directly.

cards.meaning: to repeat exactly what someone else has written or said
cards.example: You can quote the author directly if you use citation.

cards.meaning: to explain the meaning of something
cards.example: Interpret the data in your own words.

cards.meaning: to assess the quality or value of something
cards.example: Evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of each approach.

cards.meaning: to combine information from different sources
cards.example: Synthesize the key ideas from all the readings.

cards.meaning: to arrange items into groups based on common features
cards.example: Classify the vocabulary items by topic.

cards.meaning: to state the exact meaning of a word or idea
cards.example: Define the key terms before beginning the analysis.

cards.meaning: to show or prove something clearly
cards.example: Use examples to demonstrate your point.

cards.meaning: to present reasons in support of a position
cards.example: She argued persuasively for stricter environmental laws.

cards.meaning: to cause someone to believe or do something through argument
cards.example: The essay aims to persuade the reader to act.

cards.meaning: to tell a story or account of events
cards.example: The memoir narrates her journey from poverty to success.
